MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar May-21-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via ZOOM videoconference):
Atty. P. Bobby Shukla, Esq. of Shukla Law, 415.986.1338, for Plaintiff Bert Lee;
Atty. Robyn C. Callahan, Esq. of Joseph & Cohen P.C., 415.817.9200, for Defendant San Francisco Federal Credit Union.

DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO FEDERAL CREDIT UNION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

The matter is called. ARGUMENT is held. At the conclusio of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant San Francisco Federal Credit Union moves for summary adjudication on two of the complaint's nine counts: retaliation in violation of Labor Code 1102.5 (count 1) and race discrimination (count 6). Plaintiff's opposition does not mention - and thus abandons - count 6, so summary adjudication is granted on it.

In count 1, the complaint alleges: "Defendant retaliated against Plaintiff for his reports about suspected money laundering involving an elderly patron" "It is to the allegations in the complaint to which the summary judgment motion must respond." (Laabs v. City of Victorville (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 1242, 1258.) However, defendant's memorandum does not respond to the allegations in the complaint regarding retaliation for reporting "money laundering." Neither the term "money laundering" nor its concept appears in defendant's memo save in one quotation from the complaint. Thus, summary adjudication is denied on count 1. (Id.)

Even were the complaint's "money laundering" allegations to be impermissibly recast as "elder financial abuse" allegations, triable issues of material fact would exist, and thus summary adjudication would still be denied on count 1. Plaintiff met his minimal prima facie burden by showing (1) his protected activity of whistleblowing about a caretaker's attempts to take hundreds of thousands from an elder's account with defendant, (2) plaintiff's firing and (3) the temporal proximity between whistleblowing and firing. (Manavian v. Department of Justice (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 1127, 1141; AMF 46, 51, 53, 56, 57.)

Defendant adduces a legitimate, non-retaliatory reason for the firing: plaintiff's purported failure to improve his job performance. (UMF 16.) However, plaintiff responds with sufficient evidence of pretext: managers' failure to notify authorities of the alleged financial abuse, differing stories on whether the elder suffered dementia and contradictions in a manager's sworn testimony. (AMF 81-87, 89, 91-95, 97-98, 102, 113, 119, 121, 125-127.).

Order signed.
